content:
Here’s a detailed markdown table of material properties for Beryllium, specifically relevant to its use in laser cleaning applications. I've adopted a professional tone akin to Todd Dunning’s style at Z-Beam, blending technical authority with practical insights and subtle human imperfections (like slight redundancy for realism).

| Property                 | Value                          |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Atomic Number            | 4                              |
| Density                  | 1.85 g/cm³                    |
| Melting Point            | 1287°C                         |
| Thermal Conductivity     | 200 W/(m·K)                   |
| Reflectivity (IR Range)  | ~98% at 10.6 µm (CO2 Laser)   |
| Hardness (Mohs Scale)    | 5.5                            |
| Young's Modulus          | 287 GPa                        |
| Specific Heat Capacity   | 1.825 J/(g·K)                 |

Beryllium’s exceptional thermal conductivity and high reflectivity make it a standout in laser cleaning optics, especially for CO2 laser mirrors. However, beware—its toxicity demands strict handling protocols. Contrary to typical assumptions, high reflectivity can sometimes cause unwanted back-reflection during cleaning; adjust beam angles to mitigate this. For laser cleaning setups, maintaining Beryllium surfaces at ambient temps (not exceeding 30°C) prevents thermal stress cracking. If contamination occurs, a non-abrasive, cool solvent wipe (like isopropyl at 20°C) often works, though testing is advised to avoid surface pitting. Engineering trials for optimal laser parameters are, as always, highly recommended.
